title: Request access for Security + GRC to Audit Log Coverage Dashboard (least-privilege roles)
preview:
Requesting access for Security and GRC to the new “Audit Log Coverage (Sensitive Actions)” dashboard used for evidence collection and ongoing monitoring.

Business need
- SOC 2 / ISO-aligned evidence: demonstrate monitoring + coverage tracking for sensitive actions audit events.
- Security incident response: ability to quickly validate whether expected audit events are being emitted for specific event_types/services.

Requested access (least privilege)
1) Read-only access to dashboard(s) + saved views.
2) No ability to edit dashboards, create alerts, or query raw log payloads.
3) Access should allow viewing:
   - expected event types vs observed
   - per-service coverage breakdown
   - time window filters (last 24h / 7d / 30d)
   - links out to runbook for regressions
4) Access should NOT allow:
   - exporting raw event bodies containing target identifiers or other potentially sensitive context
   - writing/modifying Grafana folders/dashboards

Users / groups
- Olga Petrov (GRC)
- Daniel Carter (Compliance evidence support)
- Naomi Feldman (Product - Security & Compliance)
- Marcus Lin (Security Eng exec owner) (optional)

Acceptance criteria
- A documented role/group exists for Security+GRC coverage dashboard viewing.
- Access is granted to the above users (or an org group) within 2 business days.
- Instructions exist for future access requests (who approves, what scope).

Notes
- This is internal-only access; not customer-facing.
- The dashboard is referenced by the coverage regression runbook and will be linked in future evidence collection checklists.

title: Activation funnel dashboard discrepancy (EU-West drop + mismatch vs warehouse) after lifecycle growth instrumentation changes
preview:
Issue summary
- Growth/Marketing and Product flagged a sudden drop and cross-source mismatch in the activation funnel metrics (signup → API key created → first request) used for the Lifecycle Growth Experiments sprint.
- Primary symptom: EU-West conversion from signup → api_key_created appeared ~12–18% lower than US-East starting 2026-01-21 18:00 UTC, and overall “new signups” appeared higher than expected vs auth/user tables.
- Impact: Week 1–2 experiment readouts risked being invalid/noisy; we paused one planned expansion of the email A/B until numbers were reconciled.

Impact
- Affected dashboards: Activation Funnel (Observability Pack) + internal Looker view used by Growth.
- Impacted decisions: stop/go thresholds and lift calculations for onboarding email v2 and console nudge experiments.
- Customer impact: none direct (reporting/measurement issue only), but could cause us to make incorrect product/GT-M decisions.

Detection
- Reported via Slack #help thread ("events missing in one region") and follow-up in #eng-platform on 2026-01-22.
- Observability Pack funnel panel showed EU-West drop; Mei Lin noted mismatch vs daily new users in warehouse.

What changed recently
- PR-28431: added/standardized activation events for signup → api key → first request → first 100k tokens.
- PR-241: new dashboard panels + funnel query definitions.
- Console nudge framework shipped behind flags; additional event properties and cohort assignment introduced during the week.

Initial hypotheses
1) Ingestion lag or partial pipeline outage in EU-West.
2) Region routing misconfiguration (EU events landing in US dataset or vice versa).
3) Event validation rejecting records due to missing required properties (user_id, event_id) for EU only.
4) Duplicate event emission inflating baseline in one region, making EU look like it dropped.

Investigation notes (high level)
- Confirmed ingestion health: Kafka consumer lag normal; no sustained drop in raw event volume by region at the edge collectors.
- Cross-checked three sources for the same window (2026-01-21 00:00–2026-01-22 23:59 UTC):
  - Raw collector logs: stable event counts in eu-west.
  - Warehouse fact_events table: stable api_key_created events in eu-west.
  - Activation Funnel dashboard: apparent eu-west drop.
- Found the discrepancy was query-side + dedupe behavior:
  - The funnel panel’s Step 1 “signup” was keyed off signup_created events and deduped by (user_id, day), but the Step 1 event was being emitted twice for a subset of flows in US-East due to a console/auth edge case.
  - EU-West had fewer duplicates, so the “conversion rate” (step2/step1) looked artificially worse in EU-West when comparing regions.
  - Additionally, the dashboard query used a join that unintentionally filtered some EU users when event_time skew occurred near midnight UTC (session boundary + region bucket), amplifying the appearance of a regional issue.

Root cause
- Duplicate emission of signup_created/signup_completed for users who hit both (a) email verification completion and (b) immediate console onboarding flow that retriggered the signup event. This was more common in US-East due to traffic mix and a feature-flag exposure path.
- Dashboard query logic for Step 1 + Step 2 used inconsistent dedupe keys (Step 1 deduped by user_id/day; Step 2 deduped by event_id) and a region/date join condition that excluded a portion of EU events at boundary times.
- Net effect: inflated Step 1 counts in US-East and undercounted Step 2 in EU-West in the visualization layer, while the warehouse raw events were largely correct.

Resolution
- Engineering fix: PR-28521 shipped to stop duplicate signup event emission; verified by before/after comparison on a canary cohort and then full rollout.
- Analytics fix: updated Activation Funnel panel query to use consistent dedupe (event_id preferred, fallback to (user_id, event_name, request_id) for older records) and removed the join condition that filtered EU events at UTC boundaries.
- Backfill: no backfill required for canonical fact tables; dashboard now recomputes accurately from warehouse. Noted that historical conversion rates for 2026-01-19 to 2026-01-22 should be interpreted with caution; we annotated the dashboard.

Validation
- Post-fix checks (2026-01-26):
  - Regional conversion rates are within expected variance (eu-west no longer showing step-change).
  - New user counts align within ~1–2% between auth table and deduped signup events.
  - Funnel step drop-offs align with expected product behavior and match secondary metrics (API key creation table).

Follow-ups
- Add a data-quality alert: spike in duplicate rate for signup_created and api_key_created by region.
- Add unit tests/contract tests for event emission idempotency in console/auth flows.
- Document dedupe keys and “source of truth” definitions in the activation metrics notes + dashboard README.

Stakeholders
- Growth: Ben Carter (experiment owner), Tyler Benson (attribution)
- Product: Marissa Cole (console), Paula Mendes (telemetry/rollouts)
- Eng/Analytics: Logan Wright (assigned), Nadia Rahman, Mei Lin

References
- Slack: #help (tracking discrepancy), #eng-platform (taxonomy + dedupe)
- Related PRs: PR-28431, PR-28521, Observability Pack PR-241

title: Route experiments: variant traffic skew in eu-west during beta (expected 50/50, observed ~90/10)
preview:
Issue summary:
- Acme AI reports that their route-level experiment in Console (beta) is heavily skewed in eu-west.
- Expected split: 50/50 between variants (control vs candidate model).
- Observed split: ~90/10 (varies by hour), affecting rollout confidence.

Impact:
- Experiment results are not interpretable; customer paused ramp due to concern that assignment is not deterministic/consistent.
- Potential product trust issue during beta.

Customer context:
- Customer: Acme AI (beta design partner)
- Deployment: Dedicated; primary traffic in eu-west
- Experiment: Route experiment on /v1/chat/completions (route_id: chat-completions-prod)

Environment:
- prod, eu-west

What customer sees:
- Console results dashboard shows variant B receiving disproportionate traffic vs configured split.
- Customer also notes that skew is more pronounced for streaming requests.

Initial hypotheses:
1) Missing/low-entropy stickiness key leading to bucket collisions (e.g., IP-based fallback behind NAT).
2) Assignment cache/config propagation inconsistency specific to eu-west.
3) Metrics attribution/aggregation issue (skew in telemetry rather than actual routing).

Requested from customer:
- Sample request IDs and headers (redacted) for a few requests that landed in the "wrong" variant.
- Confirm what stickiness key is being passed (if any) via SDK.

Links:
- Slack thread: #eng-platform "variant-skew report / missing sticky key" (internal): sources/slack/eng-platform/1736538810-variant-skew-report-missing-sticky-key.json
- Dashboard (Grafana): Route Experiments > Variant Traffic Share (eu-west) (internal link)
- Dashboard (Grafana): Experiment Attribution Drop Rate (eu-west) (internal link)

title: Console cache dashboards show 0% prefix/KV cache hits due to ingestion label-drop (cardinality guard)
preview:
Customer: Aurora Support (Dedicated)
Contact: (via shared support inbox; names redacted)

Issue summary
- Customer reports Redwood Console cache dashboards show zero cache hits (prefix cache hit-rate = 0%, KV cache reuse = 0) across all routes/models since enabling the new cache telemetry rollout.
- Customer believes caching is working (observed lower latency on repeated prompts), but Console indicates no hits and “no data” in some panels.

Impact
- Customer cannot validate cache efficiency improvements / cost optimization work.
- Risk of customer assuming regression in runtime caching.

Environment
- Dedicated deployment
- Region: us-east
- Console: Production
- Affected time range: ~2025-03-12 07:00 UTC onward (customer noticed 2025-03-12 morning PT)

What we expected
- Non-zero prefix cache reuse for repeated prompts and chat threads.
- Cache dashboards should populate within ~5–10 minutes.

Observed
- Console dashboards show:
  - Prefix cache hit-rate: 0%
  - KV cache hit-rate: 0%
  - Wasted prefill: 0 / blank
  - Evictions: blank
- Other runtime metrics (latency, tokens/sec) appear normal.

Initial hypothesis
- Telemetry present in runtime but dropped during ingestion due to new cardinality guards / label allowlist mismatch.

Steps to reproduce (internal)
1) Open Console > Observability > Cache Efficiency for Aurora Support tenant.
2) Select time range “Last 6 hours”.
3) Filter route = support-agent-chat, model = rw-llama-3.1-70b-instruct.
4) Panels show 0% hit-rate and/or no data.

Investigation notes
- Support (Owen) checked SRE dashboards for the same metrics without tenant/route filters: global cache telemetry looks healthy in us-east.
- Telemetry team (Logan / Nadia) queried ingestion drop counters and found elevated drops for cache metric families when label set included route_id + model_version + tenant_id.
- We confirmed runtime emission is happening by checking a raw scrape/stream sample in the telemetry staging mirror: counters increment for prefix_cache and kv_cache, but the corresponding time series do not appear in the downstream metrics store when filtered by tenant/route.

Root cause
- Ingestion-side cardinality guard configuration for cache metric families incorrectly classified certain label combinations as forbidden and dropped them.
- Specifically, for the cache hit-rate metrics, the guard was:
  - Allowing the metric name family, but
  - Dropping time series when route_id was present (or when route_id + model_version appeared together), because the allowlist expected route_group but the gateway was still emitting route_id for Dedicated tenants.
- Outcome: downstream metrics store contained only the heavily-aggregated series (or none, depending on query), and Console queries (which rely on route/model breakdowns) returned 0/empty.

Why this looked like “0 cache hits” (instead of “no telemetry”)
- Console query fallback behavior: when high-cardinality series are missing, some panels fall back to a numerator/denominator query that resolves to 0 due to missing denominators in that label space.
- Net effect: dashboards show 0% instead of clearly indicating “telemetry dropped”. (Follow-up opportunity: improve panel queries / add ingestion-drop banner.)

Customer-facing timeline
- 2025-03-12 10:18 PT: Customer opened ticket noting “cache hit rate stuck at zero since yesterday; is caching disabled?”
- 2025-03-12 10:42 PT: Support acknowledged; asked for timeframe, route names, and screenshot of Console filters.

Comments / updates

2025-03-12 (Owen Phillips, Support)
- Acknowledged receipt. Asked customer to confirm whether they changed routes/models and whether they pinned a new model version.
- Internal note: Similar symptom seen during rollout when label guard rules were too strict. Looping in Telemetry.

2025-03-12 (Logan Wright, Telemetry)
- Seeing ingestion drop spikes for cache.* metrics correlated with route_id label for a handful of tenants. Likely allowlist mismatch.
- Requesting example label sets from Console query.

2025-03-12 (Felix Schneider, Console)
- Confirmed Console is querying by tenant + route + model_version for these panels. If that time series is dropped, the panel may misleadingly show 0%.

2025-03-13 (Nadia Rahman, Telemetry)
- Identified config issue: cache metrics policy expects route_group; dedicated gateway still emits route_id for this tenant. Guard drops route_id as “high cardinality.”
- Proposed mitigation: map route_id -> route_group at ingestion for cache families OR expand allowlist to accept route_id only when it matches a bounded allowlist from control plane.

2025-03-13 (Ethan Park, Platform)
- Dedicated tenants use stable route IDs but still too many for global allowlist. Suggest we allow route_id only for a bounded set per-tenant via config, else fallback to route_group.

Fix implemented
- Updated cardinality guard configuration for cache metric families to:
  - Accept route_group as the preferred breakdown label.
  - For tenants where only route_id exists, perform ingestion-time relabeling: route_id -> route_group (using gateway/control-plane mapping), and drop raw route_id for the cache metric families.
  - Preserve model and model_version labels per the cache telemetry policy.
- Change rolled out via observability-pack update (see related PR) and applied to prod ingestion in us-east.

Validation
- Post-fix checks (2025-03-13 18:30 UTC):
  - Ingestion drop counter for cache metric families returned to baseline.
  - Console cache dashboards for Aurora Support populated with non-zero hit-rate (prefix cache hit-rate fluctuating 22–38% on support-agent-chat route; KV reuse visible).
  - SRE dashboards confirm time series exist for route_group + model_version label space.

Customer communication (final)
- Informed customer this was a telemetry ingestion/configuration issue (not caching disabled).
- Confirmed dashboards should backfill going forward; historical period during the drop window will not fully backfill at route granularity.

Resolution
- Status set to Resolved after customer confirmed dashboards show expected non-zero cache hits.

Follow-ups
1) Console: Add panel state/annotation when ingestion-drop is detected for requested label space to avoid “0%” confusion. (Owner: Felix Schneider)
2) Telemetry: Add automated test/QA check for cache metrics label policy mismatch (route_id vs route_group) during rollout. (Owner: Leila Farouk)
3) Platform/Gateway: Accelerate migration to emit route_group consistently across deployments per tagging plan. (Owner: Amara Diallo)

Workaround (if encountered again)
- Temporarily query cache metrics at higher aggregation (model only, no route filter) to verify runtime caching behavior while ingestion rules are corrected.

Attachments
- Customer screenshot (Console cache dashboard showing 0%) attached in Jira (not included here).
- Internal query snippets + drop counter graphs linked in internal comments.

title: Console route experiment results appear stale / not updating for active experiment (aggregation + propagation lag suspected)
preview:
Customer reports that the new Console "Route Experiments" results dashboard is not updating (appears stuck on the same deltas/graphs) even though traffic is flowing to both variants.

Issue summary:
- Console results view for an active route experiment shows stale charts/metrics (latency/cost/error deltas unchanged for ~45–90 minutes).
- Customer validated traffic is being split (they see variant headers in responses).

Impact:
- Customer cannot make ramp/stop decisions from Console due to stale guardrail readouts.
- Increased risk of running an experiment longer than intended / missing regression.

Customer context:
- Account: Acme AI (beta design partner)
- Experiment: route-level A/B test (A=baseline, B=model swap) on production route.
- Customer expectations: near-real-time results (they assumed <5–10 min lag).

Environment:
- Prod
- Regions observed: us-east and eu-west

What we think is happening (initial hypotheses):
1) Config propagation lag: experiment config updates (start/ramp) not consistently visible to results attribution pipeline, causing mismatched {experiment_id, variant_id} in rollups.
2) Analytics aggregation job delay: per-variant rollup dataset behind (job stalled, backlog, or partition not advancing), causing Console to read older materialized windows.
3) Attribution drop: experiment dimensions missing for a subset of requests (especially streaming/retries), reducing signal and keeping deltas flat.

Requested by customer:
- Confirmation whether this is expected / what the typical freshness SLA is.
- Workaround guidance while we investigate.

Triage checklist (Support):
- Confirm experiment status is "Running" in Console + capture experiment_id, route_id, time range.
- Validate variant assignment is active by checking response headers (x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-variant-id) and sample 20–50 requests.
- Check freshness banner behavior in Console (if any) and whether changing time window updates numbers.
- Ask customer to provide approximate time they started/ramped the experiment and their local timezone.

Internal notes:
- Likely tied to recent schema/attribution changes during beta (see related incident and attribution drop rate work).
text:
Customer reports that the new Console "Route Experiments" results dashboard is not updating (appears stuck on the same deltas/graphs) even though traffic is flowing to both variants.

Issue summary:
- Console results view for an active route experiment shows stale charts/metrics (latency/cost/error deltas unchanged for ~45–90 minutes).
- Customer validated traffic is being split (they see variant headers in responses).

Impact:
- Customer cannot make ramp/stop decisions from Console due to stale guardrail readouts.
- Increased risk of running an experiment longer than intended / missing regression.

Customer context:
- Account: Acme AI (beta design partner)
- Experiment: route-level A/B test (A=baseline, B=model swap) on production route.
- Customer expectations: near-real-time results (they assumed <5–10 min lag).

Environment:
- Prod
- Regions observed: us-east and eu-west

What we think is happening (initial hypotheses):
1) Config propagation lag: experiment config updates (start/ramp) not consistently visible to results attribution pipeline, causing mismatched {experiment_id, variant_id} in rollups.
2) Analytics aggregation job delay: per-variant rollup dataset behind (job stalled, backlog, or partition not advancing), causing Console to read older materialized windows.
3) Attribution drop: experiment dimensions missing for a subset of requests (especially streaming/retries), reducing signal and keeping deltas flat.

Requested by customer:
- Confirmation whether this is expected / what the typical freshness SLA is.
- Workaround guidance while we investigate.

Triage checklist (Support):
- Confirm experiment status is "Running" in Console + capture experiment_id, route_id, time range.
- Validate variant assignment is active by checking response headers (x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-variant-id) and sample 20–50 requests.
- Check freshness banner behavior in Console (if any) and whether changing time window updates numbers.
- Ask customer to provide approximate time they started/ramped the experiment and their local timezone.

Internal notes:
- Likely tied to recent schema/attribution changes during beta (see related incident and attribution drop rate work).

title: Telemetry query timeouts triggered by incident-bot bursts in #incidents (v2 context cards) causing slow/empty diagnostics
preview:
Issue Summary
During several customer-impacting events and internal incident drills, incident-bot v2 telemetry context cards intermittently rendered as empty or partially complete. Slack threads showed repeated “Telemetry query timed out” messages. Investigation indicates the bot can generate bursty query patterns (multiple responders + retries + interactive actions), which saturates the telemetry query service and triggers timeouts. These timeouts are most acute under incident conditions (elevated error rates + higher on-call activity), which is exactly when the bot is relied upon.

Impact
- On-call responders lost fast access to saturation signals (GPU util / queue depth / KV cache pressure), increasing time-to-triage.
- Telemetry query service experienced elevated p95/p99 latency; in worst cases, query workers hit concurrency limits and began failing fast.
- Secondary impact: dashboards and ad-hoc queries in the same time window degraded (shared query infrastructure).

Environment
- Production (primarily us-east, eu-west)
- Affects Slack channels: #incidents, #eng-oncall
- Affected service(s): telemetry-query-service (internal), incident-bot (Slack app)

Customer / Data sensitivity
Internal issue. No evidence of customer data exposure; failures were timeouts/empty cards only.

What we observed
- Bot posts initial incident context, then multiple responders click “Refresh signals” / “Expand details” within a short window.
- Slack interactive action retries (due to ack deadline + transient network latency) caused duplicate calls.
- Bot performed per-card, per-signal queries with limited coalescing; same incident/thread triggers repeated identical queries.

Symptoms (from bot / logs)
- Slack card banner: “Saturation signals unavailable (telemetry query timed out). Showing dashboard links.”
- telemetry-query-service logs: request_id spikes and increased “deadline exceeded” counts
- p99 latency spikes correlate with incident start + the first 10 minutes of heavy responder traffic

Steps to reproduce (reliable in staging with load)
1) Start a simulated incident thread and trigger bot context cards.
2) Have 5–10 users click “Refresh” and “Expand” within 60 seconds.
3) Observe telemetry query latency and bot timeouts, particularly if the telemetry cluster is already under moderate load.

Investigation notes
- Primary driver is burst concurrency and lack of request coalescing at the telemetry-query-service boundary.
- The bot also issues some high-cost queries (wide time windows + high-cardinality labels) when the incident metadata is incomplete (missing route/service scoping), causing fallback to broader queries.
- Slack retry behavior: interactive actions must ACK quickly; when upstream query exceeds budget, the bot may retry, amplifying bursts.

Mitigation / Fix
Implemented a multi-layer approach:
1) Cache + request coalescing for telemetry queries used by incident-bot
   - Short TTL caching (target 30–120s depending on signal class)
   - Per-(incident_id, region, service, route) de-duplication to collapse identical queries
   - Serve stale-on-error during incident windows (prefer last-known-good + links)

2) Rate limits + circuit breakers on diagnostics endpoints
   - Token bucket per bot identity + per incident thread
   - Hard cap on concurrent in-flight queries per region
   - Circuit breaker: when telemetry p95 exceeds threshold, bot switches to degraded mode automatically (links + cached summaries)

3) Tighter query budgets + bounded time windows
   - Default windows reduced for “first 5 minutes” cards
   - If incident metadata lacks scoping, bot displays an action to “Select service/region” instead of fanning out

4) Perf canary for telemetry query latency (bot-critical queries)
   - Synthetic queries run continuously
   - Alerts on sustained p95/p99 regression and timeout rate

Resolution
- Rolled out caching + timeout handling improvements in incident-bot.
- Added server-side rate limits/circuit breakers in diagnostics endpoints.
- Deployed telemetry query latency perf canary and alerting.
- Verified during two internal incident simulations and one production incident: bot returned cached summaries quickly and did not amplify telemetry load.

Verification
- Success criteria:
  - Bot context card render within 3s p95 (degraded mode allowed) in prod during load tests
  - Telemetry query timeout rate < 1% for bot-critical query set (sustained)
  - No >2x increase in telemetry-query-service QPS attributable to bot refresh bursts
- Observed (post-change):
  - Bot “saturation signals” card returns within ~1–2s when cached, ~2–4s uncached; falls back to links after budget expires
  - Telemetry query p99 no longer spikes linearly with Slack action bursts

Follow-ups / Action Items
- Document query budget and allowed label sets for incident-bot (owner: Allison Grant)
- Add guardrail test to prevent introducing high-cardinality dimensions into bot queries (owner: Nadia Rahman)
- Confirm bot’s Slack retries are idempotent across all interactive actions (owner: Devon Price)
- Update on-call troubleshooting playbook for empty cards to reflect degraded-mode behavior and where to check perf canary (owner: Rafael Mendes)

Comments
2025-02-18 (Miles Thornton): Creating ticket after multiple reports that incident-bot v2 saturation cards are timing out during bursts in #incidents. We need a fix before expanding beta beyond SRE rotation.

2025-02-18 (Logan Wright): Looking at telemetry-query-service. Seeing concurrency spikes + deadline exceeded clustered around bot activity. Suspect missing cache/coalescing. Will propose TTL caching and a per-thread rate limit.

2025-02-19 (Jacob Stein): From bot logs, Slack interactive actions retried when ack arrives late. We’re not fully idempotent on refresh path; duplicates are generating identical telemetry queries.

2025-02-20 (Nadia Rahman): Confirmed some queries are unbounded when route/service labels missing. Recommend forcing scoping in bot UI or using safe defaults with narrow windows.

2025-02-24 (Logan Wright): Implemented server-side throttles + circuit breaker config in diagnostics endpoints. Initial load test: QPS is capped; bot falls back to cached context + links.

2025-03-03 (Aisha Bello): Adding perf canary to catch regressions earlier; alert will page obs-oncall only (not #incidents) to avoid noise.

2025-03-06 (Miles Thornton): Verified during drill. Cards load reliably; when telemetry is slow, we get cached summary and deep links. Closing as Resolved.

title: Add SDK guardrail: detect 'Bearer ' prefix in api_key and warn across SDKs
preview: Background: Support saw multiple customer-reported auth issues due to key/header formatting across SDKs (SUP-4821, SUP-4884, SUP-4902, SUP-4933). Proposal: add a shared validation behavior across SDKs: (1) if api_key value begins with 'Bearer ' strip prefix and log warning (2) if trailing CR/LF exists, trim and warn (3) if other whitespace exists, error with actionable message. Also update docs and support playbook.
text: Background: Support saw multiple customer-reported auth issues due to key/header formatting across SDKs (SUP-4821, SUP-4884, SUP-4902, SUP-4933). Proposal: add a shared validation behavior across SDKs: (1) if api_key value begins with 'Bearer ' strip prefix and log warning (2) if trailing CR/LF exists, trim and warn (3) if other whitespace exists, error with actionable message. Also update docs and support playbook.
